verdict: FAIL — codex adversarial review confirmed a regression introduced by the fix: bash_context_strip.py treats killall/pkill/kill as non-shell binaries and strips their quoted arguments. This allows real dangerous commands like `killall "happy"`, `pkill -f "claude"`, and `kill "-9" 1234` to bypass the hook (exit 0 instead of exit 2). All 16 AC pytest tests and 26 unit tests pass, but the fix introduces a quoted-arg bypass not covered by any AC.

qa.user_verbatim_complaint: Fix false positives where danger tokens (killall, pkill, kill, rm) appear in quoted string arguments to non-shell binaries (e.g. codex exec with a prompt body containing 'killall' and 'claude').
qa.overall_assessment: All 16 AC tests pass and the primary incident case (AC13) is fixed. However codex adversarial review surfaced a regression: the bash_context_strip.py library treats killall/pkill/kill as non-shell binaries and strips their quoted arguments, creating a quoted-arg bypass for the very rules the fix was intended to protect. This is a band-aid pattern per Step 3 (threshold lowering / validation skip). The AC test suite did not include quoted-arg variants of the dangerous commands, so this bypass was not caught by any AC.

complaint_element_pointer.how_located: Four danger-token rules updated by dev

checks.name: TEST_INCOMPLETE sentinel check
checks.detail: grep -r 'TEST_INCOMPLETE' tests/generated/20260522-080646-A/ returned no output. Zero sentinels remain in all 16 test files.

checks.name: pytest generated tests (16 files)
checks.detail: python3 -m pytest tests/generated/20260522-080646-A/ -v: 16 passed in 4.13s. All AC1-AC16 tests pass.

checks.name: pytest unit tests (hooks/tests/test_bash_safety_context.py)
checks.detail: python3 -m pytest hooks/tests/test_bash_safety_context.py -v: 26 passed in 0.03s. Note: final implementation has 26 tests, dev report said 23 — additional tests cover DollarVariableRegression class. No issue.

checks.name: AC13 live spot-check (primary incident case — must exit 0)
checks.detail: echo '{"tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"codex exec \"The hook documentation says killall and claude processes are monitored\""}}' | bash hooks/pretool-bash-safety.sh; EXIT_CODE: 0. The primary false-positive is fixed.

checks.name: AC1 live spot-check (kill -9 bare — must exit 2)
checks.detail: EXIT_CODE: 2. BLOCKED: kill with signals is forbidden. Unquoted dangerous kill still blocked correctly.

checks.name: AC15 live spot-check (Layer 1.A regression — must exit 2)
checks.detail: Cannot invoke live directly (outer hook in QA environment intercepts 'systemctl restart happy-daemon-dev' — the very protection being tested fires on the QA command itself, confirming it works). AC15 pytest test (subprocess stdin invocation) passed in C2. Layer 1.A code (lines 560-579) runs on raw $COMMAND before the context-stripping shim at line 720; structurally confirmed. The outer hook blocking the QA's own invocation is independent live evidence that Layer 1.A is active.

checks.name: M2 binding check (compound rule line 773)
checks.detail: Read hooks/pretool-bash-safety.sh lines 769-778. Line 773: first grep uses COMMAND_CONTEXT_STRIPPED (danger-token check); second grep uses raw COMMAND (workflow-path check). Contract honored exactly as specified. Comment at line 770-772 documents this explicitly.

checks.name: Codex adversarial review — finding 1 (BLOCKER): quoted args to dangerous commands stripped
checks.detail: bash_context_strip.py treats killall, pkill, and kill as non-shell binaries and strips their quoted arguments. Verified live: killall "happy" -> exit 0 (expected 2), pkill -f "claude" -> exit 0 (expected 2), kill "-9" 1234 -> exit 0 (expected 2). The stripping logic: killall "happy" -> killall "", pkill -f "claude" -> pkill -f "", kill "-9" 1234 -> kill "" 1234. The danger-token patterns (killall|pkill)\s+.*(happy|claude|docker) and kill[-\s] no longer match the stripped form. This is a regression: the fix creates a quoted-arg bypass for the exact rules it was intended to protect.
checks.proposed_fix_from_codex: bash_context_strip.py must not strip quoted arguments to commands that are themselves the danger tokens (killall, pkill, kill, rm). These commands' arguments ARE the dangerous payload, not non-executable string content. The strip should only apply to commands that are genuinely non-shell binaries used to INVOKE payloads (codex, git, python3, etc.).

checks.name: Codex adversarial review — finding 2 (OUT OF SCOPE): bash -lc not detected as shell interpreter
checks.detail: bash -lc 'rm /tmp/x' exits 0 when expected 2. The strip does not recognize -lc as a shell-interp flag (only recognizes exact -c). ACs only test bash -c (AC5) and sh -c (AC4), not combined flags like -lc, -ec, -euxc. This is beyond the literal scope of AC4/AC5 but is a real implementation gap.

checks.name: Codex adversarial review — finding 3 (OUT OF SCOPE): heredoc piped to /bin/bash not detected
checks.detail: cat <<'EOF' | /bin/bash ... exits 0 when expected 2. The heredoc consumer detection only matches '| bash', not '| /bin/bash', '| sudo bash', etc. AC7 tests '| bash' which passes, but the absolute-path form escapes. Beyond literal AC7 scope but a real implementation gap.

blockers.title: bash_context_strip.py strips quoted args to dangerous commands, creating bypass
blockers.description: The fix treats killall, pkill, and kill as non-shell binaries and strips their quoted arguments from COMMAND_CONTEXT_STRIPPED. Commands like `killall "happy"`, `pkill -f "claude"`, `kill "-9" 1234` now exit 0 (allowed) instead of exit 2 (blocked). This is a regression introduced by the fix itself. The danger-token patterns in pretool-bash-safety.sh rely on the arguments to match (e.g., 'killall\s+.*(happy|claude|docker)'); stripping the arguments prevents the match.
blockers.reproduction: python3 -c "import subprocess, json; r = subprocess.run(['bash', 'hooks/pretool-bash-safety.sh'], input=json.dumps({'tool_name':'Bash','tool_input':{'command':'killall \"happy\"'}}).encode(), cwd='/dev/shm/dev-workspace/dot-claude', capture_output=True); print(r.returncode)" -- returns 0 (expected 2)
blockers.root_cause: hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py _strip_segment_args() at lines 259-267 is called for every command word that is not recognized as a shell interpreter. Since 'killall', 'pkill', and 'kill' are not in the shell-interp set (bash/sh/zsh/etc), their arguments are stripped. The fix should exclude the dangerous-command set from stripping.
blockers.fix_approach: Add an exclusion list in bash_context_strip.py: commands in DANGER_COMMANDS = {'killall', 'pkill', 'kill', 'rm', 'mv'} should NOT have their arguments stripped — their arguments are the payload the hook rules check against.

out_of_scope_observations.observation: Shell interpreter detection only matches exact '-c' flag, not combined forms like '-lc', '-ec', '-euxc'. This causes bash -lc 'rm /tmp/x' to not be recognized as a shell-interp invocation, so the rm is stripped rather than exposed as executable.
Heredoc consumer detection matches '| bash'/'| sh' but not '| /bin/bash', '| /usr/bin/bash', '| sudo bash', etc. Absolute-path shell consumers cause heredoc body to be treated as non-shell content.

codex_consult.feedback_summary: Codex (gpt-5.5, xhigh reasoning) identified 3 issues: (1) BLOCKING — bash_context_strip.py strips quoted args to dangerous commands (killall/pkill/kill), allowing 'killall "happy"', 'pkill -f "claude"', 'kill "-9" 1234' to bypass; (2) OUT OF SCOPE — bash -lc flag combination not detected as shell interp; (3) OUT OF SCOPE — heredoc consumer '/bin/bash' (absolute path) not detected. All three confirmed by live subprocess tests. Finding 1 is a confirmed regression and blocker. Findings 2-3 are out of scope per literal ACs but noted as implementation gaps.
codex_consult.feedback_incorporated: Finding 1 classified as in_scope_real_bug and escalated to blocker B1. Findings 2-3 classified as out_of_scope and recorded in out_of_scope_observations[].

summary.pass_rate: 16/16 AC tests pass, 26/26 unit tests pass
summary.blocker_description: bash_context_strip.py strips quoted args to killall/pkill/kill, creating a bypass for the exact rules the fix was designed to protect

failed_criteria: AC3 spirit (killall with quoted target should block)
AC regression: quoted-arg form of dangerous commands bypasses hook
critical_issues: bash_context_strip.py must not strip quoted arguments to commands in the danger set {killall, pkill, kill, rm, mv}. These commands' arguments are the dangerous payload. The strip logic should only apply to genuinely non-shell binaries used to INVOKE payloads (codex, git, python3, etc.).
Add exclusion list: if command_word in DANGER_COMMANDS: do not call _strip_segment_args(). The stripped view for 'killall "happy"' must remain 'killall "happy"', not 'killall ""'.
Add AC tests for: killall "happy" (must exit 2), pkill -f "claude" (must exit 2), kill "-9" 1234 (must exit 2).
refined_context.recommended_approach: In hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py, define DANGER_COMMANDS = frozenset({'killall', 'pkill', 'kill', 'rm', 'mv', 'rmdir'}). In _strip_segment_args() or _strip_non_shell_quoted_args(), check if the command word is in DANGER_COMMANDS and if so, return the segment unchanged instead of stripping. Add 3 new AC tests for the quoted-arg dangerous command cases. Re-run all 16 ACs plus new tests.
refined_context.additional_context: The fix correctly handles AC13 (codex exec with killall in prompt body). The regression only affects the case where killall/pkill/kill/rm ARE the command word AND their arguments happen to be quoted. The fix confuses 'dangerous token in non-shell arg' with 'dangerous command with quoted arg'.
